skill.name: routeros-firewall
skill.description: Helps create and review MikroTik RouterOS firewall filter, NAT, mangle, address-list, and interface-list configurations.
skill.summary: Generate and review RouterOS firewall rules with RouterOS-specific ordering, NAT, mangle, and DoS guidance.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, network, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns. Manual review found markdown RouterOS examples, private example addresses, RouterOS syntax, and documentation links rather than host-executed code or malicious behavior. No prompt injection evidence was found, but generated firewall configuration can affect production routing and access.

low_findings.title: Router Configuration Output Requires Review
low_findings.description: The skill teaches RouterOS commands that can drop traffic, remove tagged firewall entries, redirect DNS, add NAT, or alter policy routing. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but users must review generated commands before applying them to a router.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples are explicit RouterOS configuration changes. They are not malicious, but applying them without review could affect firewall policy or routing.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to markdown code fences, inline RouterOS command text, and RouterOS configuration examples. No host shell, Ruby backtick execution, or executable script was found in the scanned files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are markdown references with RouterOS snippets. There is no evidence of a runnable host-language file or command execution path.

low_findings.title: Network Literal Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP and URL findings are private example subnets, default route notation, and MikroTik documentation links. I found no outbound request code or data exfiltration behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses are example network configuration values and the URLs are documentation references. No code performs network access.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography flag maps to RouterOS psd port scan detection syntax, not a cryptographic primitive. The reconnaissance flags map to RouterOS find expressions and matcher value lists, not host system discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are RouterOS firewall syntax in documentation. I found no cryptographic implementation or host reconnaissance code.

content.value_statement: RouterOS firewall syntax has ordering and matcher rules that general AI tools often miss. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to produce safer MikroTik firewall, NAT, mangle, and address-list configurations.
actual_capabilities: Explains RouterOS top-to-bottom firewall rule evaluation.
Generates filter rules using connection states, address lists, and interface lists.
Drafts NAT patterns for port forwarding, DNS redirect, and masquerade.
Guides mangle-based policy routing with routing marks and DNS exemptions.
Documents DoS patterns using psd, connection-limit, address-list timeouts, and tarpit.
Highlights common RouterOS mistakes such as unsafe bulk removal and FastTrack conflicts.
limitations: Does not connect to a router or apply configuration changes.
Does not validate commands against the live RouterOS version.
Examples use placeholder interfaces, subnets, and comments that must be adapted.
IPv6 firewall configuration is out of scope except for warnings.

use_cases.title: Create a Small Office Firewall
use_cases.description: Build input and forward rules using trusted management lists, WAN interface lists, and safe rule ordering.

use_cases.title: Add Controlled NAT Rules
use_cases.description: Draft port forwarding, DNS redirect, and masquerade rules with clear comments and placement guidance.

use_cases.title: Plan Policy Routing
use_cases.description: Route selected clients through an alternate gateway while exempting DNS and avoiding FastTrack conflicts.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Input Firewall
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single WAN interface.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create RouterOS input-chain firewall rules for a router with ether1 as WAN and bridge as LAN. Allow established and related traffic, allow management from 192.168.88.0/24, drop invalid traffic, and drop other WAN input. Use comments and explain rule order.

prompt_templates.title: NAT And DNS Redirect
prompt_templates.prompt: Write RouterOS NAT rules for LAN clients behind ether1. Add masquerade for outbound traffic and redirect UDP and TCP DNS requests from LAN clients to the router. Include comments and placement notes.

prompt_templates.title: Address-List Based Access
prompt_templates.scenario: Reusable rules for multiple trusted addresses.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design RouterOS firewall rules that use an address list named trusted-mgmt instead of one rule per IP. Include idempotent cleanup using a comment prefix named audit-demo and avoid removing unrelated rules.

prompt_templates.title: Policy Routing With Mangle
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced routing through an alternate gateway.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a RouterOS v7 policy-routing pattern for subnet 10.20.0.0/24 through a VPN gateway interface. Include the routing table, DNS exemptions before marking, mangle mark-routing rules, NAT, a disabled default route, and FastTrack warnings.

output_examples.input: I need to allow management from a trusted subnet and drop other WAN input.
output_examples.output: The response should list ordered RouterOS filter rules, explain why accept rules must appear before drops, and mark each rule with a clear comment.

output_examples.input: I want selected clients to use a VPN route but keep DNS local.
output_examples.output: The response should describe a routing table, DNS exemption rules before mark-routing, mangle marks for the client subnet, NAT for the VPN interface, and a disabled route for final review.

output_examples.input: My router is under a connection flood.
output_examples.output: The response should propose address-list based detection, connection-limit thresholds, timeout behavior, and tarpit tradeoffs without claiming automatic protection.

best_practices: Use comments as ownership tags so repeated scripts remove only their own rules.
Place specific accept or exemption rules before broad drop or mark rules.
Review generated commands on a test router before applying them to production.
anti_patterns: Do not use bulk removal patterns that delete all static firewall rules.
Do not combine FastTrack with mangle-based routing marks for the same traffic.
Do not copy placeholder interface names, subnets, or gateways without adaptation.

faq.question: Does this skill apply firewall rules automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides RouterOS guidance and example commands for review.

faq.question: Can it help with NAT configuration?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers dst-nat port forwarding, DNS redirect, and src-nat masquerade patterns.

faq.question: Does it support mangle and policy routing?
faq.answer: Yes. The reference material covers routing tables, mark-routing, DNS exemptions, and FastTrack interactions.

faq.question: Is IPv6 firewalling included?
faq.answer: Only as a warning. IPv6 uses a separate RouterOS firewall path and is not fully covered.

faq.question: Why does the skill emphasize rule order?
faq.answer: RouterOS evaluates firewall rules from top to bottom, and the first terminal match decides the result.

faq.question: Should generated commands be used unchanged?
faq.answer: No. Replace example interfaces, subnets, comments, and thresholds with values from the target router.
